name: AWS IAM Root Account Active Access Keys
description: The AWS root user account has active programmatic access keys, providing unrestricted API access that bypasses all IAM policy boundaries. An attacker who obtains these long-lived keys can disable logging, delete all resources, modify IAM policies, export any data, and lock legitimate administrators out of the account. Root access keys are not subject to IAM permission boundaries or SCPs.
mitigations: Delete all root user access keys immediately — the root account should never have programmatic credentials
Replace root API automation with IAM roles scoped to least-privilege policies
Enable hardware MFA on root and restrict it to break-glass scenarios only
Set a CloudWatch alarm and SNS alert for any root account API activity
See: https://hub.prowler.com/check/iam_no_root_access_key

name: AWS IAM Root Account No Hardware MFA Device
description: The AWS root user account has no MFA enabled, or uses a virtual MFA device instead of a hardware token. Without hardware MFA, a stolen root password enables full account takeover. Virtual MFA is weaker than hardware because device backup codes and authenticator app compromise allow second-factor bypass. An attacker with root access can delete all resources, change policies, and disable monitoring.
mitigations: Assign a hardware MFA token (FIDO2 security key or hardware TOTP device) to the root account
Deactivate any virtual MFA device currently on the root account after hardware MFA is set
Keep a controlled break-glass process: document the hardware token location, require two-person authorization, and log every use
See: https://hub.prowler.com/check/iam_root_hardware_mfa_enabled

name: AWS IAM Policy Grants Full Administrative Wildcard Access
description: An IAM policy grants Action:* and Resource:* (full admin), either directly to users or via overly broad managed policies. Any principal with this policy can escalate to full account control, create backdoor IAM users, exfiltrate secrets, and disable audit logging. Wildcards make it impossible to audit what a user actually needs.
mitigations: Replace wildcard policies with service-scoped, resource-scoped least-privilege policies
Use IAM Access Analyzer to generate policies from actual CloudTrail activity
Apply Permission Boundaries on all IAM roles created by developers to cap maximum privilege
Audit inline policies and AWS managed policies attached to users — prefer role-based access
See: https://hub.prowler.com/check/iam_policy_allows_privilege_escalation

name: AWS IAM Password Policy Does Not Enforce Strong Passwords
description: The IAM account-level password policy has weak settings: minimum length below 14 characters, no requirement for uppercase, lowercase, numbers, or symbols, or allows password reuse. Weak passwords make IAM user accounts susceptible to brute-force, credential stuffing, and password spray attacks, enabling account takeover without exploiting any vulnerability.
mitigations: Set minimum password length to 14 characters and require all character classes (upper, lower, number, symbol)
Prevent password reuse for the last 24 passwords and set maximum password age to 90 days
Prefer IAM Identity Center (SSO) with federated identity over long-lived IAM user passwords
Enable MFA on all IAM users regardless of password strength
See: https://hub.prowler.com/check/iam_password_policy_minimum_length_14

name: AWS IAM User Credentials Unused for More Than 90 Days
description: An IAM user has active access keys or a console password that have not been used in over 90 days. Dormant credentials are a persistent attack surface: they may have been leaked in code repositories, sent in emails, or exfiltrated without detection. An attacker who finds stale credentials has time to quietly enumerate permissions and move laterally before triggering any alerts.
mitigations: Deactivate and delete access keys and console passwords unused for more than 90 days
Automate credential lifecycle enforcement with AWS Config rules or a scheduled Lambda
Replace long-lived IAM user credentials with IAM roles and temporary STS tokens
Set up IAM Access Analyzer findings for active credentials and unused services
See: https://hub.prowler.com/check/iam_user_unused_credentials_45days

name: AWS S3 Bucket Public Access Block Not Fully Enabled
description: An S3 bucket does not have all four S3 Public Access Block settings enabled (BlockPublicAcls, IgnorePublicAcls, BlockPublicPolicy, RestrictPublicBuckets). Without these controls, a single misconfigured bucket policy or ACL can expose the bucket's contents to the public internet. Attackers routinely scan for misconfigured S3 buckets to harvest credentials, PII, and business-sensitive data.
mitigations: Enable all four S3 Block Public Access settings at both the bucket level and the account level
Use an SCP to prevent disabling Block Public Access across the organization
Audit bucket policies and ACLs with S3 Access Analyzer to detect public exposure
Enable S3 server access logging and Amazon Macie to detect and alert on data exposure
See: https://hub.prowler.com/check/s3_bucket_public_access_block_enabled

name: AWS S3 Bucket Default Server-Side Encryption Not Enabled
description: An S3 bucket lacks a default server-side encryption (SSE) configuration, meaning objects uploaded without explicit encryption headers are stored unencrypted. If the bucket is accessed by an unauthorized party (misconfigured policy, compromised credentials, pre-signed URL leak), data is immediately readable without needing to decrypt it. SSE-KMS is preferred over SSE-S3 for audit-ability and key management control.
mitigations: Enable default SSE-KMS encryption on all buckets containing sensitive data
Use SSE-S3 as a minimum baseline for all other buckets
Enforce encryption via bucket policy: deny PutObject requests that do not specify server-side encryption
Use AWS Config rule s3-default-encryption-kms to detect non-compliant buckets
See: https://hub.prowler.com/check/s3_bucket_default_encryption

name: AWS S3 Bucket Server Access Logging Disabled
description: An S3 bucket does not have server access logging enabled. Without access logs, there is no record of who accessed which objects, from which IP, when, and using which API operation. This makes it impossible to detect unauthorized access, reconstruct data breaches, or meet audit and compliance requirements after an incident.
mitigations: Enable S3 server access logging and direct logs to a centralized, access-controlled S3 bucket
Use CloudTrail Data Events as a higher-fidelity complement for individual object-level audit trails
Set S3 lifecycle policies on the log bucket to retain logs for at least 90 days
Enable Amazon Macie on the log bucket to detect sensitive data patterns in access logs
See: https://hub.prowler.com/check/s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_enabled

name: AWS S3 Bucket Versioning Not Enabled
description: An S3 bucket does not have versioning enabled. Without versioning, a single DeleteObject or PutObject call permanently overwrites or deletes data with no recovery path. An attacker with write access (or an operator error) can destroy data irreversibly. Ransomware targeting S3 typically overwrites all objects and then deletes the originals.
mitigations: Enable S3 versioning on all buckets containing business-critical or compliance data
Use S3 Object Lock in Governance or Compliance mode to prevent version deletion
Enable MFA Delete to require multi-factor authentication before any version can be permanently deleted
Set up replication to a cross-account or cross-region bucket for disaster recovery
See: https://hub.prowler.com/check/s3_bucket_versioning_enabled

name: AWS S3 Bucket MFA Delete Not Enabled
description: S3 bucket versioning is enabled but MFA Delete is not configured. Without MFA Delete, an attacker or insider who compromises an IAM user's API keys can permanently delete all object versions, destroying all data in the bucket with no recovery path. MFA Delete is the only control that prevents version deletion by compromised programmatic credentials.
mitigations: Enable MFA Delete on buckets containing sensitive or irreplaceable data using root credentials
Combine MFA Delete with S3 Object Lock (Compliance mode) for regulatory retention requirements
Restrict s3:DeleteObjectVersion permission to break-glass IAM roles only
See: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/MultiFactorAuthenticationDelete.html

name: AWS EC2 Instance Assigned a Public IPv4 Address
description: An EC2 instance is directly reachable from the internet via a public IPv4 address. Publicly addressed instances are continuously scanned by automated tools, enabling direct probing of exposed services, brute-force of SSH/RDP, and exploitation of any unpatched service running on the instance. Management ports should never be internet-facing.
mitigations: Place EC2 instances in private subnets and expose only through application load balancers or API Gateway
Use AWS Systems Manager Session Manager for admin access instead of SSH/RDP over the internet
If a public IP is required, restrict Security Group ingress to specific known CIDR blocks only
Enable VPC Flow Logs and GuardDuty to detect anomalous traffic to public instances
See: https://hub.prowler.com/check/ec2_instance_public_ip

name: AWS EC2 IMDSv2 Not Enforced on Instance
description: An EC2 instance allows IMDSv1 (Instance Metadata Service version 1), which does not require a session token. An attacker who achieves SSRF (Server-Side Request Forgery) in an application running on the instance can directly fetch the instance's IAM role credentials from http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/ without needing any token, enabling cloud API calls with the instance's permissions.
mitigations: Enforce IMDSv2 (require session-oriented requests with PUT token) on all EC2 instances
Set aws ec2 modify-instance-metadata-options --http-tokens required on all existing instances
Use an SCP to prevent launching new instances without IMDSv2 enforcement
Apply WAF rules to detect and block SSRF attempts targeting 169.254.169.254
See: https://hub.prowler.com/check/ec2_instance_imdsv2_enabled

name: AWS EC2 EBS Volume Not Encrypted
description: An EC2 EBS volume is not encrypted at rest. If the snapshot is shared accidentally, copied to another account, or the storage layer is physically accessed, data is exposed in plaintext. Unencrypted volumes also create compliance violations for PCI-DSS, HIPAA, and SOC2 workloads. Encryption can be enforced at the account level with zero performance impact for most workloads.
mitigations: Enable EBS encryption by default at the account level in all regions
Use AWS KMS customer-managed keys (CMK) for EBS encryption on sensitive workloads
Migrate unencrypted volumes: create an encrypted snapshot, then create a new encrypted volume from it
Use AWS Config rule encrypted-volumes to continuously detect non-compliant volumes
See: https://hub.prowler.com/check/ec2_ebs_volume_encryption

name: AWS EC2 Security Group Allows Unrestricted SSH Ingress
description: An EC2 security group allows inbound TCP port 22 (SSH) from 0.0.0.0/0 or ::/0, exposing the instance to brute-force attacks, SSH key compromise attempts, and exploitation of any SSH daemon vulnerability from anywhere on the internet. Automated scanners find open SSH ports within minutes of exposure.
mitigations: Remove 0.0.0.0/0 ingress on port 22 — restrict SSH to specific admin CIDR blocks or remove it entirely
Replace SSH access with AWS Systems Manager Session Manager (no open ports required)
If SSH must be exposed, deploy an EC2 Instance Connect Endpoint or a bastion host with MFA
Enable GuardDuty SSH brute-force detection (UnauthorizedAccess:EC2/SSHBruteForce finding)
See: https://hub.prowler.com/check/ec2_securitygroup_allow_ingress_from_internet_to_ssh_port_22

name: AWS EC2 Security Group Allows Unrestricted RDP Ingress
description: An EC2 security group allows inbound TCP port 3389 (RDP) from 0.0.0.0/0 or ::/0, exposing Windows instances to brute-force, credential stuffing, and exploitation of RDP protocol vulnerabilities (BlueKeep, DejaBlue) from anywhere on the internet. RDP is one of the most targeted services in ransomware attack chains.
mitigations: Remove 0.0.0.0/0 ingress on port 3389 — restrict to specific admin CIDR blocks or disable RDP entirely
Use AWS Systems Manager Session Manager with RDP port forwarding for Windows admin access
Deploy a bastion host or VPN requiring MFA before reaching the RDP endpoint
Enable GuardDuty RDP brute-force detection (UnauthorizedAccess:EC2/RDPBruteForce finding)
See: https://hub.prowler.com/check/ec2_securitygroup_allow_ingress_from_internet_to_rdp_port_3389

name: AWS RDS Instance Publicly Accessible
description: An RDS database instance has PubliclyAccessible set to true and is reachable from the internet. A publicly exposed database is directly targetable by credential brute-force, exploitation of database engine vulnerabilities, and automated enumeration. Any single credential compromise or CVE in the database engine provides direct data access without going through the application layer.
mitigations: Set PubliclyAccessible to false on all RDS instances — route access through the application tier or VPN
Place RDS instances in private subnets with no route to an Internet Gateway
Use VPC security groups to allow database port ingress only from specific application security groups
Use RDS Proxy to pool connections and avoid exposing database credentials to application code
See: https://hub.prowler.com/check/rds_instance_no_public_access

name: AWS RDS Instance Encryption at Rest Not Enabled
description: An RDS database instance does not have encryption at rest enabled. If database storage snapshots are shared, copied cross-account, or the underlying storage is accessed outside AWS controls, data is readable in plaintext. Encryption at rest is required for HIPAA, PCI-DSS, and GDPR compliance and has no measurable performance impact on modern RDS instance classes.
mitigations: Encryption cannot be enabled on an existing unencrypted instance — take a snapshot, create an encrypted copy, restore as a new encrypted instance
Enforce RDS encryption at account level: use an SCP requiring rds:StorageEncrypted at launch
Use AWS KMS customer-managed keys for RDS encryption on sensitive workloads
Audit unencrypted snapshots with AWS Config rule rds-snapshot-encrypted
See: https://hub.prowler.com/check/rds_instance_storage_encrypted

name: AWS RDS Instance No Automated Backups
description: An RDS instance has automated backups disabled (BackupRetentionPeriod = 0). Without automated backups, there is no recovery path if the database is deleted, corrupted by a failed migration, or encrypted by ransomware. Data loss would be permanent unless a manual snapshot happened to exist before the incident.
mitigations: Enable automated backups with a retention period of at least 7 days on all production databases
Enable Multi-AZ for automatic failover and point-in-time recovery
Test restore procedures regularly — backup that has never been tested is not a backup
Use AWS Backup to centrally manage and audit backup compliance across all RDS instances
See: https://hub.prowler.com/check/rds_instance_backup_enabled

name: AWS RDS Instance SSL/TLS Not Enforced for Connections
description: An RDS instance does not enforce SSL/TLS for client connections. An attacker positioned on the network path between the application and the database (VPC misconfiguration, compromised EC2 instance, cloud-internal network tap) can intercept database queries and responses in plaintext, exfiltrating PII, credentials, or business-sensitive data.
mitigations: Set the rds.force_ssl parameter to 1 for PostgreSQL and require_secure_transport for MySQL
Download the AWS RDS CA certificate bundle and configure all application clients to verify the server certificate
Use parameter groups to enforce TLS and deploy parameter group changes via IaC to prevent drift
See: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonRDS/latest/UserGuide/UsingWithRDS.SSL.html

name: AWS KMS Customer-Managed Key Automatic Rotation Not Enabled
description: An AWS KMS customer-managed symmetric key (CMK) does not have automatic key material rotation enabled. Long-lived cryptographic key material increases the blast radius if a key is ever compromised or if a cryptanalytic weakness is discovered in the underlying algorithm. Non-rotating keys also conflict with NIST, PCI-DSS, and HIPAA rotation requirements.
mitigations: Enable automatic annual rotation on all customer-managed symmetric KMS keys
Use on-demand rotation when a key compromise is suspected rather than waiting for the annual cycle
Apply least privilege to key policies: separate key administrator and key user roles
Enable KMS CloudTrail logging to audit all cryptographic operations on each key
See: https://hub.prowler.com/check/kms_cmk_rotation_enabled

name: AWS CloudTrail Multi-Region Logging Not Enabled
description: AWS CloudTrail is either not enabled or is not configured as a multi-region trail. Regions without active trail coverage create forensic blind spots where an attacker can create IAM users, launch instances, or exfiltrate data without leaving any API audit trail. Attackers aware of unmonitored regions deliberately use them to avoid detection.
mitigations: Create a multi-region CloudTrail trail with IsMultiRegionTrail: true to capture activity in all regions
Enable CloudTrail log file integrity validation to detect tampering with log files
Deliver CloudTrail logs to a dedicated, locked-down S3 bucket with MFA delete and bucket policy preventing deletion
Forward CloudTrail logs to CloudWatch Logs and set metric filters for high-risk events (root login, MFA disable)
See: https://hub.prowler.com/check/cloudtrail_multi_region_enabled

name: AWS CloudTrail Log File Validation Not Enabled
description: CloudTrail log file integrity validation is disabled. Without it, an attacker who gains write access to the S3 log bucket can modify or delete log files to cover their tracks, and there is no cryptographic way to detect the tampering. Compliance frameworks including PCI-DSS and SOC2 require evidence of log integrity.
mitigations: Enable CloudTrail log file validation — AWS generates SHA-256 hash digests for each log file
Restrict S3 bucket access: only CloudTrail should have write access; deny all DeleteObject actions
Forward logs to CloudWatch Logs as a secondary destination that is harder to tamper with
See: https://hub.prowler.com/check/cloudtrail_log_file_validation_enabled

name: AWS CloudTrail Logs Not Encrypted with KMS
description: CloudTrail logs are stored in S3 without KMS encryption. The logs contain sensitive information: IAM role ARNs, API parameters, source IP addresses, user agent strings, and resource identifiers. An attacker who gains access to the S3 bucket can read the full API history of the account without needing additional privileges.
mitigations: Configure the CloudTrail trail to use a KMS CMK for log file encryption
Restrict the KMS key policy to CloudTrail service and authorized security auditors only
Enable S3 server-side encryption as a defense-in-depth measure in addition to CloudTrail-level encryption
See: https://hub.prowler.com/check/cloudtrail_kms_encryption_enabled

name: AWS VPC Flow Logs Not Enabled
description: One or more VPCs do not have VPC Flow Logs enabled. Without flow logs, there is no record of network-level traffic — which instances communicated with which IPs, on which ports, and whether connections were accepted or rejected. Attackers can probe, move laterally, and exfiltrate data over the network without leaving any evidence that can be used for detection or forensics.
mitigations: Enable VPC Flow Logs for all VPCs with TrafficType: ALL and deliver to CloudWatch Logs or S3
Create CloudWatch metric filters and alarms for rejected traffic patterns indicating port scanning
Integrate flow logs with a SIEM or use Amazon Detective for automated anomaly detection
See: https://hub.prowler.com/check/vpc_flow_logs_enabled

name: AWS VPC Default VPC Exists in Region
description: The AWS default VPC exists in one or more regions. The default VPC is pre-configured with an internet gateway, a public subnet in every AZ, and default security groups that allow all outbound traffic. Workloads accidentally launched into the default VPC are immediately internet-reachable, bypassing any network segmentation. Attackers who compromise a workload in the default VPC can reach the internet without additional infrastructure.
mitigations: Delete the default VPC in all regions where it is not explicitly required
Use SCPs to prevent creating resources in the default VPC or require VPC tagging
Create application-specific VPCs with private subnets and explicit routing to force intentional exposure decisions
See: https://hub.prowler.com/check/vpc_default_sg_no_rules

name: AWS GuardDuty Not Enabled in Region
description: Amazon GuardDuty threat detection is not enabled in one or more regions. Without GuardDuty, there is no automated detection of: IAM credential compromise (anomalous API calls), EC2 instance compromise (mining, C2 communication, data exfiltration), S3 data exfiltration attempts, or EKS cluster compromise. Attackers operating in unmonitored regions can operate undetected for extended periods.
mitigations: Enable GuardDuty in all regions, including regions where you have no active workloads
Enable all GuardDuty protection plans: S3 Protection, EKS Audit Logs, Lambda Network Activity, RDS Login Activity
Configure GuardDuty findings to trigger SNS notifications and Security Hub ingestion
Use AWS Organizations GuardDuty delegated administrator to enforce GuardDuty across all accounts
See: https://hub.prowler.com/check/guardduty_is_enabled

name: AWS Config Not Enabled in Region
description: AWS Config is not enabled, meaning there is no continuous record of resource configuration changes. Without Config, there is no way to determine the configuration state of resources before an incident, no compliance drift detection, and no change timeline for forensic analysis. Attackers who modify security group rules, IAM policies, or S3 bucket policies leave no configuration audit trail.
mitigations: Enable AWS Config in all regions with all resource types recorded
Deliver Config snapshots to a centralized S3 bucket with bucket policy preventing deletion
Enable AWS Config conformance packs for CIS Benchmark, PCI-DSS, or HIPAA to get pre-built compliance rules
See: https://hub.prowler.com/check/config_recorder_all_regions_enabled

name: AWS Lambda Function Assigned Administrator IAM Role
description: A Lambda function's execution role grants AdministratorAccess or equivalent wildcard permissions. If the Lambda function processes untrusted input (API events, SQS messages, S3 uploads), an attacker who achieves code execution within the function via injection or a dependency vulnerability inherits full account admin rights. Any SSRF or RCE in Lambda code with admin role equals account takeover.
mitigations: Scope Lambda execution roles to the exact services and resources the function accesses
Use IAM Access Analyzer to generate least-privilege policies from Lambda function's actual API calls
Apply resource-based policies on downstream services (S3, DynamoDB, SQS) to limit what the Lambda can do even with a broad role
Enable Lambda function URLs or API Gateway auth to prevent unauthenticated invocation
See: https://hub.prowler.com/check/awslambda_function_not_publicly_accessible

name: AWS EKS Cluster etcd Secrets Not Encrypted with KMS
description: An EKS cluster does not have envelope encryption of Kubernetes secrets enabled via AWS KMS. Kubernetes secrets (API keys, database passwords, TLS certificates) stored in etcd are base64-encoded but not encrypted. An attacker who gains access to the etcd data layer (via a compromised control plane component or AWS support access) can decode and read all cluster secrets without any cryptographic barrier.
mitigations: Enable secrets encryption at EKS cluster creation using a KMS CMK — this cannot be added to existing clusters without recreation
Use Kubernetes external secrets operator or AWS Secrets Manager to store sensitive values outside etcd
Apply RBAC policies to restrict who can read secrets in each namespace
Enable EKS control plane logging to capture secret access events
See: https://hub.prowler.com/check/eks_cluster_secrets_encrypted

name: AWS EKS Cluster API Server Endpoint Publicly Accessible
description: An EKS cluster's Kubernetes API server endpoint is publicly accessible from the internet. The API server is the control plane entry point — an attacker who obtains valid kubeconfig credentials or exploits an API server vulnerability can gain full cluster control: deploying workloads, reading secrets, pivoting to any connected AWS service via pod IAM roles, and exfiltrating data.
mitigations: Disable public endpoint access and use private endpoint only, with VPN or Direct Connect for admin access
If public access is required, restrict it to specific CIDR blocks (operator office IPs, CI/CD runner IPs)
Enable EKS audit logging and pipe to CloudWatch for detection of unauthorized API calls
Rotate all kubeconfig credentials if public API server access was previously enabled
See: https://hub.prowler.com/check/eks_cluster_endpoint_public_access

name: AWS ECR Container Image Scanning on Push Not Enabled
description: An ECR repository does not have image scanning on push enabled. Containers with known vulnerabilities (CVEs in OS packages, language runtimes, or application libraries) are pushed and deployed without any vulnerability check. An attacker who can exploit a known CVE in a deployed container image can compromise the running workload, pivot to other containers, or escalate via the host.
mitigations: Enable ECR Enhanced Scanning (powered by Amazon Inspector) on all repositories
Block deployment of images with CRITICAL or HIGH CVEs using ECR lifecycle policies or deployment pipeline gates
Use ECR image immutability to prevent overwriting tagged images
Integrate ECR scanning results with Security Hub for centralized vulnerability management
See: https://hub.prowler.com/check/ecr_repository_scan_on_push_enabled

name: AWS ELB/ALB HTTPS Not Enforced for Listener
description: An Elastic Load Balancer or Application Load Balancer has an HTTP listener without automatic redirect to HTTPS. User traffic (authentication tokens, session cookies, form data, API credentials) is transmitted in plaintext, making it interceptable by a network-positioned attacker, corporate proxy, or ISP. Any credential transmitted over HTTP is compromised the moment the request is sent.
mitigations: Add an HTTPS listener with a valid ACM certificate and set up an HTTP-to-HTTPS redirect rule
Enable HSTS headers on the application to instruct browsers to always use HTTPS
Use a TLS security policy that disables TLS 1.0 and 1.1 — prefer ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-2021-06
See: https://hub.prowler.com/check/elbv2_insecure_ssl_ciphers

name: AWS Secrets Manager Secret Automatic Rotation Not Configured
description: A secret in AWS Secrets Manager does not have automatic rotation enabled. Long-lived, static credentials (database passwords, API keys) that are never rotated create a permanently open window if they are leaked. Credentials exposed in logs, git commits, or API responses remain valid indefinitely until manual rotation is performed.
mitigations: Enable automatic rotation with an appropriate rotation interval (30 days for sensitive credentials, 90 days for others)
Use managed rotation for RDS, Redshift, DocumentDB, and ElastiCache to have AWS handle the rotation Lambda
Audit all secrets with rotation disabled and prioritize rotation of credentials with production access
See: https://hub.prowler.com/check/secretsmanager_automatic_rotation_enabled

name: AWS ElastiCache Cluster Encryption in Transit Not Enabled
description: An ElastiCache cluster (Redis or Memcached) does not require TLS for client connections. Cache data (session tokens, PII, application secrets cached for performance) is transmitted in plaintext between the application and the cache tier. A VPC-internal attacker who compromises another resource in the same VPC can perform passive interception or active MITM on cache traffic.
mitigations: Enable in-transit encryption on ElastiCache clusters — requires node restart on existing clusters
Use ElastiCache Redis AUTH token and require TLS for all client connections
Place ElastiCache in a dedicated security group allowing ingress only from application security groups
See: https://hub.prowler.com/check/elasticache_replication_group_encryption_in_transit_enabled

name: AWS DynamoDB Table Point-in-Time Recovery Not Enabled
description: A DynamoDB table does not have Point-in-Time Recovery (PITR) enabled. Without PITR, a ransomware attack, application bug, or misconfigured migration that deletes or corrupts table data has no recovery path short of restoring from a manually created backup (which may be days or weeks old). PITR enables restoration to any second within the last 35 days.
mitigations: Enable PITR on all production DynamoDB tables — it has minimal cost impact and no performance overhead
Use AWS Backup to take additional scheduled full backups to a cross-account backup vault
Enable DynamoDB Streams for change-data-capture if audit trails of individual item changes are needed
See: https://hub.prowler.com/check/dynamodb_tables_pitr_enabled

name: AWS CloudWatch Alarm for Root Account Activity Not Configured
description: There is no CloudWatch metric filter and alarm configured to alert on root account API activity. Any action taken with root credentials (key usage, console login, MFA change) should trigger an immediate alert. Without this alarm, root account abuse — whether by an attacker or unauthorized insider — may go undetected until a forensic investigation is triggered by other means.
mitigations: Create a CloudTrail metric filter for root account usage and a CloudWatch alarm with SNS notification
Filter on eventName: ConsoleLogin with userIdentity.type: Root or any API call by root
Send alerts to an on-call security channel and require acknowledgment within 15 minutes
Use AWS Security Hub CIS Benchmark finding 3.3 to detect this control gap automatically
See: https://hub.prowler.com/check/cloudwatch_log_metric_filter_root_usage
